summary: AI reliability issues stem from three separate architectural challenges that keep getting lumped into the same category. Prompt engineering alone can't fix them. But the sourcing and verification frameworks media organizations have used for centuries translate into clear engineering solutions developers can implement...

sections.overview: AI reliability issues stem from three separate architectural challenges that keep getting lumped into the same category. Prompt engineering alone can't fix them. But the sourcing and verification frameworks media organizations have used for centuries translate into clear engineering solutions developers can implement today.вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»їп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»їп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»їп»ївЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»їп»ївЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»їп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊп»ївЂ‹вЂЊп»їп»ївЂЊп»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»їп»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»їп»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊп»ївЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹п»ївЂЊвЂ‹п» email_summary: AI reliability issues stem from three separate architectural challenges that keep getting lumped into the same category. Prompt engineering alone can't fix them.

title: Build a local voice agent with Red Hat OpenShift AI
summary: Writing a voice agent can be a daunting task. There are many moving parts to designing and developing a functional voice agent. In this post, I will explain how to to create a functional Red Hat pizza shop voice agent using Red Hat OpenShift AI, focusing on practical architecture choices and implementation lessons learned along the way. Like a lot of journeys you might take, the fun is not only...
timeline_entries: Writing a voice agent can be a daunting task. There are many moving parts to designing and developing a functional voice agent. In this post, I will explain how to to create a functional Red Hat pizza shop voice agent us

Whisper was an easy selection for speech-to-text processingвЂ”it is a widely known model from OpenAI with a quantized version available on Hugging Face from Red Hat AI and performed well on initial testing.

Text to speech was much harder to nail down. When I started, models like Qwen-3 TTS had not yet been released. I plan to test additional options now that more models are available. I found a well-performing model from Hi

Table 1: Model selection for the voice application. Model name License Description Notes Whisper apache-2.0 Speech-to-text Quantized version of openai/whisper-large-v3-turbo whisper-large-v3-turbo-quantized.w4a16 Higgs-A
Initial testing involved recording a WAV audio file and submitting a POST request to the /v1/audio/transcriptions API endpoint on the Whisper model.

Table 2: GPU STT generation speed comparison. Card details Cores AMI instance type gen x NVIDIA L4 (24 GB) 7,424 CUDA cores, 240 Tensor Cores (Gen 4), 60 RT Cores (Gen 3) g6-8xlarge 0.78 NVIDIA L40S (48 GB) 18,176 CUDA c
Calling the TTS model was initially as simple as using curl and piping the output into ffmpeg and ffplay to hear the output on my laptop. This was handy for quickly testing the Higgs Audio model.

sections.overview: Writing a voice agent can be a daunting task. There are many moving parts to designing and developing a functional voice agent. In this post, I will explain how to to create a functional Red Hat pizza shop voice agent using Red Hat OpenShift AI, focusing on practical architecture choices and implementation lessons learned along the way.
sections.technical_breakdown:
This deployment ran a single-node OpenShift cluster running in AWS with a hardware accelerator featuring 24 GB of memory (m6i.4xlarge).

I started with four agentsвЂ”the supervisor agent that holds the session state for the pizza order, and which then routes to one of the other agents depending on the structured output received.

 def build_graph():
 """Compile and return the LangGraph instance (with checkpointer for interrupts)."""
 graph = StateGraph(SupervisorState)
 graph.add_node("supervisor", supervisor_command_node)
 graph.add_node("order_agent", order_agent_node)
 graph.add_node("pizza_agent", pizza_agent_node)
 graph.add_node("delivery_agent", delivery_agent_node)

 # Interrupt nodes
 graph.add_node("wait_for_user_after_pizza", wait_for_user_after_pizza)
 graph.add_node("wait_for_user_after_order", wait_for_user_after_order)
 graph.add_node("wait_for_user_after_delivery", wait_for_user_after_delivery)

 graph.add_edge(START, "supervisor")
 return graph.compile(checkpointer=MemorySaver()) In this case:

 I have a pizza agent that helps the user choose a pizza.

The improvement stemmed from the hardware's increased allocation of CUDA and Tensor Cores rather than total memory capacity.

In code, the interrupt nodes serve as simple placeholders for now.

 def wait_for_user_after_pizza(state: SupervisorState) -> Command:
 """Interrupt after pizza agent, waiting for user's next input."""
 user_text = interrupt(_interrupt_payload(state, "pizza_agent"))
 return Command(
 goto="supervisor", update={"messages": [HumanMessage(content=str(user_text))]}
 ) Observability LLM systems generate a high volume of token interactions.

Capture complex agent interactions and traces with MLflow observability tools.
sections.impact:
If gen x consistently remains greater than or equal to 1.0, any remaining playback degradation stems from client-side runtime issues. Figure 5: Client user metrics. After processing, a TTS model converts the generated response back into spoken audio, and a transport layer manages moving the audio data between the client and the server backend. Well, I initially overlooked this step and spent a lot of time writing client application code to patch choppy audio playback, when all I needed was better GPU hardware for my TTS model.

 (gen x) = (audio seconds produced) / (wall clock seconds elapsed) This measurement tells us the key truth:

 If the gen x ratio drops below 1.0 for significant periods, the TTS stream is generating slower than real time.

Classified impact band: high. Affected layer signal: cloud вЂ” aws.
sections.resolution: Upgrading to a higher-capacity hardware accelerator resolved the choppy playback.
sections.lessons_learned:
In this post, I will explain how to to create a functional Red Hat pizza shop voice agent using Red Hat OpenShift AI, focusing on practical architecture choices and implementation lessons learned along the way.

So my first lesson learned was about agent handoffs.

Well, I initially overlooked this step and spent a lot of time writing client application code to patch choppy audio playback, when all I needed was better GPU hardware for my TTS model.

 (gen x) = (audio seconds produced) / (wall clock seconds elapsed) This measurement tells us the key truth:

 If the gen x ratio drops below 1.0 for significant periods, the TTS stream is generating slower than real time.

The improvement stemmed from the hardware's increased allocation of CUDA and Tensor Cores rather than total memory capacity.
sections.root_cause_highlight:
Likely root-cause theme: deployment failure. Because of vLLM and standard OpenAI endpoints, I can use Red Hat OpenShift AI to continue experimenting, measuring, and testing newer models in my voice agent system. This deployment ran a single-node OpenShift cluster running in AWS with a hardware accelerator featuring 24 GB of memory (m6i.4xlarge). To recap, this deployment demonstrates how to:

 Run a voice-sandwich pattern in a local environment without external Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) voice services.

sections.trends:
Large-scale outages remain the forcing function for resilient architecture, communication, and automation investments.

Public postmortems compress organizational learning into patterns the broader industry can reuse.

Observability shifts remain tightly coupled with incident response quality and capacity planning accuracy.

Demand spikes and capacity cliffs continue to drive autoscaling, queueing, and graceful degradation designs.

title: Gang autoscaling on OpenShift with Kueue and ProvisionRequest
summary: Running high-performance workloads like AI/ML training, high-performance computing (HPC) simulations, or large data processing on Kubernetes introduces a critical challenge: gang scheduling and coordinated autoscaling.
timeline_entries: This post explores how combining Red Hat build of Kueue, a queueing and resource management tool, with the ProvisionRequest API brings true gang autoscaling capabilities to OpenShift, so your critical workloads start eff
Configuring OpenShift autoscaler to support ProvisionRequest Edit the feature gate for OpenShift clusters to be in DevPreviewNoUpgrade .
maxNodesTotal: 24
unneededTime: 5m

name: your_machine_set_you_want_to_scale Configuring Red Hat build of Kueue to work with autoscaling Red Hat build of Kueue acts as the intelligent arbiter for resource requests, managing job queues and making sure clust

To achieve this, Kueue must recognize when a job requires provisioning new capacity by setting the appropriate cluster queue and resource flavor configurations. The following breakdown represents the complete plumbing re

ResourceFlavor: Defining available resource types This resource is the simplest piece. It defines a type of resource. In a real-world scenario, you might have one for spot-instances and one for on-demand . Here, itвЂ™s jus
controllerName: kueue.x-k8s.io/provisioning-request

sections.overview: Running high-performance workloads like AI/ML training, high-performance computing (HPC) simulations, or large data processing on Kubernetes introduces a critical challenge: gang scheduling and coordinated autoscaling. Imagine you have a distributed machine learning job that requires ten worker pods to start simultaneously. If the cluster only has capacity for eight, traditional Kubernetes scheduling might start those eight pods, leaving the remaining two unschedulable.
sections.technical_breakdown:
Note The values in this example CR are for demonstration purposes and should only be used after consulting a cluster administrator.

 apiVersion: "autoscaling.openshift.io/v1"
kind: "ClusterAutoscaler"
metadata:
 name: "default"
spec:
 podPriorityThreshold: -10
 resourceLimits:
 maxNodesTotal: 24
 cores:
 min: 8
 max: 128
 memory:
 min: 4
 max: 256
 logVerbosity: 4
 scaleDown:
 cordonNodeBeforeTerminating: Enabled
 enabled: true
 delayAfterAdd: 10m
 delayAfterDelete: 5m
 delayAfterFailure: 30s
 unneededTime: 5m
 utilizationThreshold: "0.4"
 scaleUp:
 newPodScaleUpDelay: "10s"
 expanders: ["Random"] The next step is to configure the MachineAutoscaler to know which MachineSet resources you need to scale.

Finally, retryStrategy defines that if the cloud provider lacks capacity, the system will attempt to provision the nodes up to two times before giving up.

 kind: ProvisioningRequestConfig
metadata:
 name: prov-test-config
spec:
 provisioningClassName: best-effort-atomic-scale-up.autoscaling.x-k8s.io
 managedResources:
 - cpu
 retryStrategy:
 backoffLimitCount: 2 How the components flow together To understand how these individual configurations interact in a live cluster, you can track the end-to-end lifecycle of a job request as it passes through the provisioning pipeline.

The maxReplicas field in this CR controls the maximum number of nodes that this MachineAutoscaler will be able to create.

 apiVersion: "autoscaling.openshift.io/v1beta1"
kind: "MachineAutoscaler"
metadata:
 name: "worker-autoscaler"
 namespace: "openshift-machine-api"
spec:
 minReplicas: 1
 maxReplicas: 12
 scaleTargetRef:
 apiVersion: machine.openshift.io/v1beta1
 kind: MachineSet
 name: your_machine_set_you_want_to_scale Configuring Red Hat build of Kueue to work with autoscaling Red Hat build of Kueue acts as the intelligent arbiter for resource requests, managing job queues and making sure cluster capacity is reserved or provisioned before jobs are admitted.

If the cluster only has capacity for eight, traditional Kubernetes scheduling might start those eight pods, leaving the remaining two unschedulable.

By enabling true gang autoscaling, we eliminate resource waste, prevent scaling deadlocks, and make sure that complex, multi-pod jobsвЂ”like distributed AI/ML trainingвЂ”can start immediately and reliably once all required capacity is available.
sections.impact:
The following breakdown represents the complete plumbing required to move from a job request to physical cloud infrastructure, connecting the user's queue to an automated provisioning system. It tells Kueue that even if the user has enough quota (fewer than 36 CPUs), do not admit the job until the check named sample-prov returns a Ready status. First, a user submits a job to the user-queue (the LocalQueue ). We are exploring how to use these same mechanisms to handle sudden, large spikes in demand for AI/ML model serving, allowing the system to scale rapidly to meet real-time user needs without maintaining expensive idle capacity.

Classified impact band: medium. Affected layer signal: platform вЂ” kubernetes.
sections.resolution:
Note The values in this example CR are for demonstration purposes and should only be used after consulting a cluster administrator.

 apiVersion: "autoscaling.openshift.io/v1"
kind: "ClusterAutoscaler"
metadata:
 name: "default"
spec:
 podPriorityThreshold: -10
 resourceLimits:
 maxNodesTotal: 24
 cores:
 min: 8
 max: 128
 memory:
 min: 4
 max: 256
 logVerbosity: 4
 scaleDown:
 cordonNodeBeforeTerminating: Enabled
 enabled: true
 delayAfterAdd: 10m
 delayAfterDelete: 5m
 delayAfterFailure: 30s
 unneededTime: 5m
 utilizationThreshold: "0.4"
 scaleUp:
 newPodScaleUpDelay: "10s"
 expanders: ["Random"] The next step is to configure the MachineAutoscaler to know which MachineSet resources you need to scale.
sections.lessons_learned:
This coordinated approach prevents resource waste so your latency-sensitive, high-throughput workloads get exactly the resources they need right from the start.

By enabling true gang autoscaling, we eliminate resource waste, prevent scaling deadlocks, and make sure that complex, multi-pod jobsвЂ”like distributed AI/ML trainingвЂ”can start immediately and reliably once all required capacity is available.

title: Building Agents that Don't Break Themselves
summary: Building agents is fun. Rebuilding agents that break themselvesвЂ¦ less so. A lot of Fly people are building agents with less of a penchant for self-destruction by teaching their agents to do anything risky in a Sprite. You get an agent that stays alive long enough to actually use its snazzy self-improvement features, and you can allow your agent to try things that would otherwise be...
timeline_entries: Building agents is fun. Rebuilding agents that break themselvesвЂ¦ less so. A lot of Fly people are building agents with less of a penchant for self-destruction by teaching their agents to do anything risky in a Sprite. Yo

If you re going to steal any part of Henrique s design it should be this one: SpriteDoc runs flyctl inside the sandbox authenticated as the actual user, but the user s token is never written to the Sprite. It is injected

Security always guides our architectural decisions (right), but few among us can claim to have never skirted around security best practice in the interest of saving time. That s why it s worth demonstrating how much time

sections.overview: Building agents is fun. Rebuilding agents that break themselvesвЂ¦ less so. A lot of Fly people are building agents with less of a penchant for self-destruction by teaching their agents to do anything risky in a Sprite.
sections.technical_breakdown:
It s a long-lived process that only becomes more competent and less stupid if memory, skills and history persist.

Every session runs on one shared server, in one Node.js runtime.

Every user s commands run as themselves, against their own resources, with their own permissions, and no long-lived secret ever lands on shared disk.

An agent that can roll back is an agent you can actually let run unattended, because the worst case is restore and retry instead of restore from backup, if you have one.
sections.impact:
SpriteDoc is multi-user and built on top of the Pi agent. Every user s shell would sit in the same process the agent itself runs in. Sprites spin up fast enough that it s all but unnoticeable as a user. Every command after that runs in that same sandbox, isolated from the agent and from every other user.

Classified impact band: medium. Affected layer signal: general production.
sections.resolution: Resolution detail may be partial in the RSS excerpt. Refer to the original write-up for timelines, mitigations, and verification steps.
sections.lessons_learned:
You get an agent that stays alive long enough to actually use its snazzy self-improvement features, and you can allow your agent to try things that would otherwise be battleship-scale footguns.

Where SpriteDoc spins up a throwaway sandbox per session, Hermes does the opposite with the same building block; it keeps one Sprite per task and resumes it next time, so everything it installed last session is still there.
